In positions like this, keeping an important pawn protected while improving your king is usually easier and safer than moving the rook again. How to find it: Use this simple checklist before choosing a move: first ask, 'Do I have any important pawn far up the board?' Here, yes — the pawn on d6. Then ask, 'Can my king improve while defending it?' Ke6 answers both questions at once, so choose that kind of useful king move over a less direct rook move.
body.takeaway: In simplified positions, look for king moves that defend your advanced pawns and become more active at the same time.

body.coaching:
Move: h6  
Why: Your endgame has no undefended pieces demanding immediate defense, so you do not have to spend the tempo on something like Ne2 defending f4. h6 uses the active kingside pawn while keeping both central pawns fixed. The key point is structure versus activity: if you push f5 too early, you commit the center immediately; h6 improves your outside passer first without changing the central tension yet. How to find it: In an endgame where nothing is hanging, run this routine before pushing a central pawn: 1) ask whether any piece needs urgent protection; here, none do. 2) identify your main asset — here, the advanced kingside pawns. 3) compare pawn pushes by commitment: f5 changes the center right away, while h6 advances your passed-side pawn without releasing the central tension. That comparison leads you to h6.
body.takeaway: When there is no tactical emergency in an endgame, improve your passed pawns before committing the central pawn structure unless the tactic clearly demands otherwise.
